disease.name: Breastfeeding Jaundice
disease.note: Early-onset exaggerated physiological jaundice in breastfed neonates due to inadequate milk intake leading to dehydration, caloric deprivation, and decreased stooling. Distinguished from breast milk jaundice which is later onset. Onset day 2-5 of life. Managed by improving breastfeeding technique and frequency, not by stopping breastfeeding.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Bilirubin above phototherapy threshold -> inpatient phototherapy; above exchange transfusion threshold -> EMERGENCY

subtypes.name: Mild Breastfeeding Jaundice
subtypes.notes: Bilirubin below phototherapy threshold, managed with feeding optimization alone

subtypes.name: Severe Breastfeeding Jaundice
subtypes.notes: Bilirubin at or above phototherapy threshold, requiring phototherapy

firstLine.test: Blood type and Rh of mother and infant
Bhutani nomogram plot for gestational age
Weight assessment and percent weight loss from birth

investigations.goldStandard: Fractionated bilirubin with Bhutani nomogram risk stratification; clinical assessment of feeding adequacy

emergency: Exchange transfusion if bilirubin exceeds exchange transfusion threshold per AAP Bhutani nomogram
Intensive phototherapy (double/triple) until safe level reached
IVIG 1g/kg if isoimmune hemolysis and bilirubin rising despite phototherapy
inpatient: Phototherapy at appropriate threshold per AAP gestational age-based nomogram
Lactation consultant support and breastfeeding optimization
Supplement with expressed breast milk or formula if inadequate intake
Weigh daily, monitor input/output and bilirubin every 6-12 hours
outpatient: Close follow-up with bilirubin check within 24-48 hours
Breastfeeding education: proper latch, frequent feeding 8-12 times/day
Early follow-up for weight check in 2-3 days
Maternal reassurance and education about warning signs
preventive: Early and frequent breastfeeding within first hour of life
Lactation support in hospital and at home
Routine weight monitoring in first week
Bilirubin screening before discharge with risk stratification
Early follow-up within 48-72 hours of discharge

disease.pathophysiology: Inadequate breastfeeding intake in first days of life -> decreased milk volume -> relative dehydration -> reduced stooling frequency and meconium passage -> decreased enterohepatic clearance of bilirubin (meconium contains bilirubin that is normally excreted). Additionally, caloric deprivation impairs hepatic UGT1A1 enzyme conjugation activity. The result is exaggerated physiological jaundice as bilirubin accumulates from both increased enterohepatic circulation and decreased conjugation. Onset is earlier (day 2-5) compared to breast milk jaundice (day 5-14). The condition is managed by improving breastfeeding adequacy, not by discontinuing breastfeeding.

complications.complication: Poor weight gain and failure to thrive
Maternal breastfeeding cessation due to anxiety/pressure

mustNotMiss: Bilirubin appearing in first 24 hours -> hemolytic disease until proven otherwise
Direct/conjugated hyperbilirubinemia >2 mg/dL or >20% of total -> biliary atresia or neonatal hepatitis
Signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (lethargy, poor feeding, high-pitched cry, opisthotonos)
Family history of G6PD deficiency or previous neonatal jaundice in siblings
ABO or Rh incompatibility requiring IVIG

diagnosticClues.clue: Jaundice onset at day 2-5 in an exclusively breastfed infant with poor intake
Decreased urine output (<4 wet diapers/day by day 4)
Decreased stool frequency (meconium not fully passed by day 3-4)
Primiparous mother with breastfeeding difficulties/latch concerns
Late preterm infant (34-37 weeks) with breastfeeding challenges
Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with negative Coombs test and normal blood group

exclusionClues.clue: Jaundice appearing in first 24 hours of life
Positive direct Coombs test (DAT) indicating isoimmune hemolysis
Direct bilirubin >2 mg/dL or >20% of total (conjugated jaundice)
Onset after day 5-7 of life (consider breast milk jaundice or sepsis)
